text.title: Model Evaluation — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the model-evaluation methodology. Sources: Zheng et al. — MT-Bench / LLM-as-judge (arXiv:2306.05685); Liang et al. — HELM holistic evaluation (arXiv:2211.09110); Anthropic — model evaluation guidance.

rule.statement: Define 2–3 primary metrics per task type and pass/fail thresholds before running any evaluation; do not chase metrics post-hoc.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc threshold setting is p-hacking with steps: every eval will 'pass' on the metric the engineer picked after seeing the result.

rule.statement: Build evaluation datasets from real production queries (≥200 samples per task); never rely purely on synthetic data.

rationale.source: Liang et al. — HELM holistic evaluation (arXiv:2211.09110)

rule.rationale: Synthetic data understates the long-tail and the adversarial inputs that drive real-world quality regressions.

rule.statement: Use multiple judge models (e.g. GPT-4 + Claude) and average scores; report inter-judge agreement (Cohen's κ) alongside the score.
rule.rationale: LLM-as-judge is biased toward outputs that look like the judge's family; averaging across families is the only correction that survives audit.

rule.statement: Score offline on a frozen suite first; only promote to A/B testing on live traffic after offline pass; never let online traffic be the first eval.
rule.rationale: Offline catches the structural failures; online catches the distribution-shift failures. Skipping offline burns customer trust for cheap signal.

rule.statement: Sample production output and run lightweight eval continuously (faithfulness + cost + latency); alert on >5% drop from the previous 7-day window.
rule.rationale: Model providers patch their models; without continuous sampling, the first signal of a quality drop is a customer escalation.
